title: Molokhia with chicken
subtitle: Egyptian jute leaf soup with garlic and coriander
excerpt: Frozen molokhia leaves simmered in chicken broth, finished with a sizzling garlic and coriander fry that goes in at the last moment. The leaves thicken the soup to a slightly viscous consistency. Eaten over rice or with bread, with the poached chicken on the side.
sourceNotes: Molokhia (also mulukhiyah, melokheya) is one of the oldest Egyptian dishes, with references stretching back to antiquity. The jute leaf produces a slightly mucilaginous soup when cooked, valued in Egypt for its texture. Regional variations exist across Egypt, Lebanon, Tunisia, and West Africa. The Egyptian version uses chicken broth and the garlic-coriander fry called ta'leya.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the soup and chicken separately from the rice.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The chicken broth can be made ahead. Add the molokhia fresh when serving.

content.text: Frozen molokhia is sold in blocks at Middle Eastern and African food shops. Defrost at room temperature for an hour before using, or add the frozen block directly to hot broth — it will break apart as it heats. Do not overcook the molokhia once added: it thickens the soup quickly and extended cooking makes it more viscous than intended.

The ta'leya (the garlic and coriander fried in butter) goes in at the very end, directly into the hot soup, and is stirred through immediately. The sizzle and fragrance of garlic hitting hot fat is the signature moment of making molokhia. It must be done just before serving; the garlic loses its punch if added too early.

Put the {{chicken-thigh}} and {{onion}} in a large pot with 1.5 litres of cold water and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Bring to a boil, skim the foam, then simmer for 25 minutes until the chicken is cooked through. Remove the chicken and set aside. Strain the broth. Return 1 litre of broth to the pot and bring to a simmer. Add the defrosted {{molokhia-frozen}} and stir. Simmer for 5 minutes until the molokhia has thickened the soup.

Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} in a small pan over medium-high heat until foaming. Add the {{garlic}} and {{coriander-ground}} and cook for 30 to 60 seconds until the garlic is just golden and very fragrant. Pour the ta'leya immediately into the molokhia soup and stir through. Taste and adjust salt. Serve the soup over rice, with the reserved {{chicken-thigh}} pieces on the side. Offer lemon wedges and a small bowl of wine vinegar with chopped onion if available.

items.fix: Add the molokhia and simmer for 5 minutes only. Extended cooking increases the mucilage. Some viscosity is correct and desirable — excessive viscosity is not

items.cause: Heat too high or cooked too long
items.fix: The garlic needs only 30 seconds in the hot butter. It continues cooking from the residual heat once poured into the soup. Aim for golden, not brown
Simmer the chicken broth uncovered for 10 minutes to reduce and concentrate it before adding the molokhia

content.text: Molokhia is considered Egypt's national dish by many Egyptians, eaten across all social classes and at every occasion from weeknight dinners to celebration meals. The combination of the thickened jute leaf soup with rice, chicken, and the sharp ta'leya garnish is quintessentially Egyptian. The Fatimid Caliph who reportedly banned it for its aphrodisiac properties only increased its cultural resonance. Outside Egypt, molokhia is cooked throughout the Arab world in varying versions: the Lebanese version keeps the leaves whole; the Tunisian version uses dried leaves; the Egyptian version is always the chopped frozen variety poured into a broth.
